title: Spanish wave marbling
subtitle: The undulating wave pattern produced by a paired comb pass on a nonpareil base
excerpt: The Spanish wave is a flowing S-curve pattern that fills the sheet in repeating vertical bands. Like the bouquet, it begins with a nonpareil base, but the final pass uses a wide comb drawn in alternating left-right zigzag strokes down the length of the tray rather than a single straight stroke. The result is an undulating wave that crosses the full width of the sheet.
sourceNotes: Charles Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1853), chapter on wave and Spanish patterns. The Spanish wave is documented as a standard pattern in Woolnough's manual and in the marbling copybooks of the Bibliothèque nationale de France. Pre-1928.

content.text: builds on the same nonpareil base used for the bouquet pattern. Where the bouquet pass is a single straight stroke down the tray, the

alternates left and right as it advances. The comb moves forward a short distance, shifts 2 to 3 cm to the left, moves forward again, shifts to the right, and repeats. The resulting motion traces an S-curve through the nonpareil, gathering its columns into flowing wave bands. The technique requires a steady, deliberate hand and consistent shift distances; inconsistent shifts produce irregular waves.

items.name: Marbling paints, 4 to 5 colours, prepared and tested
Fine comb for the nonpareil base (5 to 8 mm tooth spacing)
Wide comb for the wave pass (tooth spacing double the fine comb)
Newspaper strips for skimming between sheets

content.text: Follow the same steps as the bouquet pattern tutorial to produce the nonpareil base: drop colours in horizontal stripes, comb across the width, comb down the length. The Spanish wave requires a well-defined nonpareil; use 4 to 5 colours with good contrast for the most visible wave result.
Position the wide comb at the near end of the tray, teeth aligned between the nonpareil rows (same starting position as for the bouquet pass).
Advance the comb 4 to 5 cm into the tray (forward, away from you). Then shift the entire comb 2 to 3 cm to the left without lifting the teeth from the surface.

Advance another 4 to 5 cm. Shift 2 to 3 cm to the right. Repeat: advance, shift left, advance, shift right, all the way to the far end of the tray. The pattern of advance-and-shift should be as consistent as possible: equal advance distances, equal shift distances. Practise the motion with the comb above the tray before inserting it.
Lay the alum-coated paper on the surface, lift after 30 seconds, rinse gently, and dry flat.

attrs.intro: Problems that appear in the wave comb pass.

items.fix: Mark the comb handle with a pencil at intervals matching the intended advance distance. Count aloud while making each shift.
items.cause: Unequal advance distances between shifts, or shifts of varying size.
items.symptom: Waves are inconsistent in width or spacing

items.fix: Keep the comb teeth at the same surface depth during both the advance and the shift. The teeth must not leave the surface until the pass is complete.
items.cause: Comb lifted from the surface during the shift movement.
items.symptom: Wave pattern tears or blurs at the shift points

attrs.heading: Common problems with the Spanish wave
